text: One need only look as far as their shoes to know that the field must be a vital part of any student’s study of political conflict. Nonetheless, as many of us know, theIRB can be quite the barrier to carrying out this critical fieldwork. In this article, the author, reflecting on their research on the Maoist conflict in India, highlights the difficulties inherent in carrying out social science field research while navigating bureaucratic Institutional Review Boards. The author highlights three tensions present in this uneasy relationship: 1) the inappropriate application of clinical, Western ethics to non-Western societies; 2) a divergence between procedural and practiced ethics; and 3) a persistent problem of communication and mistrust between committees and researchers in the field. As a result of these barriers to meaningful engagement in the review process and over-exploration of the potential risks to participants, the decisions of review committees can obstruct meaningful ethnographic research of high social value and compel scholars to modify their primary research methods, which may be what local participants want to take part in and what scholars have the competence to carry out. To mitigate some of the ill effects of these broader bureaucratic changes, the author proposes greater communication and understanding between committees and researchers, speedier review for certain types of research that are generally deemed to carry low risk, as well as advocacy from professional academic societies to influence and shape policy as it develops on the issues of new regulatory frameworks in the field of the social sciences.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
